hints.nominative: While 'Bellum' looks the same in nominative, here it is the object of 'prosecutus est'
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'bello' — 'Bellum' is accusative, direct object
hints.vocative: Vocative is not used here — 'Bellum' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'belli' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'belli' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'belli' with -i is 2nd declension

steps.reveal: Gallicum = Gallic (acc. neut. sg., agreeing with 'Bellum')

hints.nominative: While the neuter form looks the same, 'Gallicum' agrees with 'Bellum' as accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Gallici' — 'Gallicum' is accusative modifying 'Bellum'
Genitive would be 'Caesaris' — 'Caesar' is nominative, the subject
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Caesarem' — 'Caesar' is nominative here
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Caesare' — 'Caesar' is nominative, the subject

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Caesaris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Caesaris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Caesaris' ends in -is

steps.reveal: prosecutus = having pursued/carried on (nom. masc. sg.)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'prosequens' — 'prosecutus' is perfect (deponent)
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'prosequendus' — 'prosecutus' is perfect participle
hints.future active participle: Future active participle would be 'prosecuturus' — 'prosecutus' is perfect

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'prosecutus est' translates to?

hints.to prosecute (legally): The English 'prosecute' derives from this verb, but in Latin 'prosequi' means 'to pursue/carry on'
hints.to proceed: That would be 'procedere' — 'prosequi' means 'to pursue/carry on'
hints.to protect: That would be 'protegere' — 'prosequi' means 'to pursue/carry on'

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'prosequitur' — 'prosecutus est' with participle + 'est' is perfect
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'prosecutus erat' — 'prosecutus est' is perfect
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'prosecutus sit' — 'prosecutus est' is indicative

hints.sequor, sequi, secutus sum: These are the parts of 'sequi' (to follow) — 'prosequi' is its compound pro + sequi
hints.persequor, persequi, persecutus sum: These are the parts of 'persequi' (to pursue thoroughly) — different prefix
hints.prosicio, prosicere, prosieci, prosiectu: These are not standard Latin parts — 'prosequi' is a deponent verb

translation: Caesar carried on the Gallic War
